title: Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter
source: Appendices/Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter.md
html: appendices/Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter.html
text: Explain when to use Electronic, Electronic First Curtain Shutter (EFCS), and Mechanical shutter on the Canon R5.

title: Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter
source: Appendices/Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter.md
html: appendices/Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter.html
text: Shutter mode controls how the exposure starts and ends. Electronic uses sensor readout, EFCS starts electronically and ends mechanically, and Mechanical uses physical curtains.

title: Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter
source: Appendices/Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter.md
html: appendices/Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter.html
text: Electronic shutter reads the sensor line by line. EFCS begins exposure without first-curtain movement and ends with the mechanical second curtain. Mechanical shutter uses both physical curtains.

title: Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter
source: Appendices/Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter.md
html: appendices/Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter.html
text:
- Electronic: silent shooting, no shutter shock, fastest burst options.
- EFCS: low vibration with broad compatibility.
- Mechanical: best compatibility for flash, motion, and artificial light.

title: Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter
source: Appendices/Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter.md
html: appendices/Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter.html
text:
- Electronic can show rolling shutter with fast movement.
- Electronic can band under flickering lights.
- EFCS can affect bokeh at very high shutter speeds.
- Mechanical is louder and can add vibration.

title: Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter
source: Appendices/Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter.md
html: appendices/Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter.html
text: Use Electronic for quiet subjects with controlled movement. Use EFCS for general handheld shooting. Use Mechanical for flash, fast action under artificial light, and when rolling shutter risk matters.

title: Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter
source: Appendices/Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter.md
html: appendices/Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter.html
text: Avoid Electronic shutter for fast lateral motion, flash, or flickering light. Avoid EFCS at very high shutter speeds when bokeh quality is critical.

title: Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter
source: Appendices/Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter.md
html: appendices/Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter.html
text: Need flash: Mechanical. Need silence: Electronic if motion/light allow. General stills: EFCS. Fast action under uncertain light: Mechanical.

title: Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter
source: Appendices/Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter.md
html: appendices/Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter.html
text:
- Birds in Flight: Mechanical or Electronic only after testing rolling shutter.
- Fireworks: Mechanical.
- Sports: Mechanical unless silent shooting is mandatory.
- Wildlife: Electronic for quiet moments, Mechanical for fast movement.

title: Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter
source: Appendices/Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter.md
html: appendices/Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter.html
text: On the Canon R5, Electronic shutter is silent but has rolling-shutter and flash limitations. Mechanical and EFCS remain safer default choices for mixed lighting and flash.

title: Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter
source: Appendices/Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter.md
html: appendices/Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter.html
text: Test each shutter mode with the actual subject speed and lighting. Watch backgrounds and straight vertical lines for skew.

title: AF Cases & Tracking Behavior
text: Explain Servo AF behavior, tracking response, and subject detection choices for moving subjects.

title: AF Cases & Tracking Behavior
text: AF tracking controls how quickly the camera follows subjects, ignores distractions, and switches targets.

title: AF Cases & Tracking Behavior
text: Servo AF continuously updates focus. Tracking sensitivity, Accel/Decel Tracking, and subject switching tune how the camera responds to subject movement and interruptions.

title: AF Cases & Tracking Behavior
text:
- Eye detection improves people and animal focus accuracy.
- Animal detection helps wildlife and birds.
- Vehicle detection supports motorsports and similar subjects.
- Servo AF keeps moving subjects in focus.

title: AF Cases & Tracking Behavior
text:
- Too much sensitivity can jump to distractions.
- Too little sensitivity can cling to the wrong subject.
- Subject detection can fail with blocked, small, or low-contrast subjects.

title: AF Cases & Tracking Behavior
text: Use Servo AF for moving subjects. Use subject detection when the subject type is clear. Use AF-ON/back-button focus when separating focus from shutter release helps.

title: AF Cases & Tracking Behavior
text: Avoid aggressive tracking settings in cluttered scenes. Avoid subject detection when the camera repeatedly chooses the wrong target.

title: AF Cases & Tracking Behavior
text: Predictable subject: moderate tracking. Erratic subject: increase Accel/Decel support. Busy background: reduce subject switching and use a smaller AF area.

title: AF Cases & Tracking Behavior
text:
- Birds in Flight: Servo AF, animal detection, Expand AF Area.
- People: Servo AF or One-Shot depending on movement, eye detection.
- Sports: Servo AF, tuned tracking sensitivity.
- Wildlife: Servo AF, animal eye detection when available.

title: AF Cases & Tracking Behavior
text: Canon R5 AF cases and subject detection work together. Subject detection is powerful, but AF area selection still matters.

title: AF Cases & Tracking Behavior
text: Start with a familiar AF area and change one tracking parameter at a time.

title: AF Cases & Tracking Behavior
text:
- Letting the camera switch subjects too easily.
- Using too small an AF area for erratic subjects.
- Assuming eye detection replaces good panning and framing.

title: AF Cases & Tracking Behavior
text:

title: Metering Modes
text: Explain Evaluative, Partial, Spot, and Center-weighted metering and when each mode helps.

title: Metering Modes
text: Metering determines how the camera reads scene brightness to recommend or set exposure.

title: Metering Modes
text: Evaluative analyzes the whole frame. Partial reads a central area. Spot reads a small central area. Center-weighted averages the frame with priority near the center.

title: Metering Modes
text:
- Evaluative: best general-purpose choice.
- Partial: useful when the subject is central and backlit.
- Spot: precise measurement of a small tone.
- Center-weighted: predictable for classic centered compositions.

title: Metering Modes
text:
- Evaluative can be influenced by subject recognition and scene contrast.
- Partial and Spot require careful placement.
- Center-weighted can under- or over-expose off-center subjects.

title: Metering Modes
text: Use Evaluative by default. Use Partial or Spot for difficult contrast. Use Center-weighted when you want a stable, traditional meter pattern.

title: Metering Modes
text: Avoid Spot unless you know exactly which tone you are metering. Avoid Center-weighted for off-center subjects unless exposure lock is used.

title: Metering Modes
text: General scene: Evaluative. Backlit central subject: Partial. Small known tone: Spot. Simple centered scene: Center-weighted.

title: Metering Modes
text:
- Camera Defaults: Evaluative.
- Fireworks: Manual exposure; metering is secondary.
- Landscape: Evaluative with exposure compensation as needed.
- People: Evaluative or Partial for backlight.
- Wildlife: Evaluative, with compensation for bright/dark subjects.

title: Metering Modes
text: Canon Evaluative metering is highly capable and usually the safest starting point on the R5.

title: Metering Modes
text: Use exposure compensation when the scene is mostly bright or mostly dark.

title: Metering Modes
text:
- Using Spot accidentally.
- Forgetting exposure compensation for snow, dark stages, or backlight.
- Treating the meter as a creative decision instead of a starting point.

title: Metering Modes
text:

title: Focus Bracketing & In-Camera Depth Compositing
text:
Focus Bracketing captures a sequence of photographs while the camera automatically moves focus from near to far. It is used when one photograph cannot hold enough depth of field.
Depth Compositing is the process of combining those bracketed photographs into one image where more of the subject appears sharp. Focus Bracketing creates the source frames. Depth Compositing creates the final stacked image.
Multiple focused images often produce better results than stopping down to very small apertures. At f/16, f/22, or smaller, diffraction can soften fine detail even though depth of field increases. A stack shot around f/5.6-f/11 often keeps sharper detail and creates deeper apparent focus.
Use this appendix as a field guide for estimating Focus Bracketing settings with minimal trial and error.
Table of contents:

title: Focus Bracketing & In-Camera Depth Compositing
text:
Focus Bracketing takes multiple frames focused at different distances. The camera begins at the focus point you choose, then steps focus farther away for each frame.
Depth Compositing uses the sharp regions from those frames to create a deeper-focus image. The composite can be made in camera when supported, or later in software such as Canon Digital Photo Professional, Photoshop, Helicon Focus, or Zerene Stacker.

title: Focus Bracketing & In-Camera Depth Compositing
text:
- Compatible lenses: Use Canon AF lenses that support electronic focus control. RF lenses generally work best. Many adapted EF lenses work well, but focus step behavior varies by lens.
- RAW/JPEG behavior: Keep the source images for maximum quality. In-camera depth compositing may create a processed composite file, while external software can use the original RAW frames.
- Tripod recommendations: Use a tripod for macro, product, landscape, architecture, and any stack where framing must stay fixed.
- Handheld limitations: Handheld bracketing can work for casual close-ups, but framing shifts and subject movement increase stacking errors.
- Flash compatibility: Flash can work, but recycle time must keep up with the bracket sequence. Manual flash with consistent power is usually better than variable automatic flash.
- Electronic shutter considerations: Electronic shutter can reduce vibration, but watch for banding under artificial light. Mechanical shutter may be safer when flash is required.
- Image Stabilization considerations: Use IS for handheld stacks. Turn IS off on a solid tripod for static subjects unless the lens/body combination handles tripod detection cleanly.

title: Focus Bracketing & In-Camera Depth Compositing
text:
The camera needs a starting focus point, a focus increment, and a number of shots.
Start focus on the nearest important detail. The sequence moves focus farther away from that point. If you start too deep into the subject, the near edge will never be sharp.
The key relationships are:
- Higher magnification gives shallower depth of field.
- Closer subject distance gives shallower depth of field.
- Wider aperture gives shallower depth of field.
- Smaller focus increments increase overlap between slices.
- More shots increase total depth coverage.

title: Focus Bracketing & In-Camera Depth Compositing
text:
- Creates deeper apparent focus without relying on very small apertures.
- Preserves fine detail by avoiding diffraction-heavy settings.
- Excellent for macro, product photography, flowers, food, small collectibles, and landscapes with close foregrounds.
- Lets the photographer choose sharpness and depth separately.
- External stacking software can produce high-quality results from RAW source frames.

title: Focus Bracketing & In-Camera Depth Compositing
text:
- Requires static subjects or very little movement.
- Wind can ruin flowers, insects, and foreground landscape elements.
- Flash recycle time can interrupt the sequence.
- Focus breathing can change framing between frames.
- Too few images leave gaps; too many images waste storage and time.
- In-camera compositing is convenient but less controllable than external software.

title: Focus Bracketing & In-Camera Depth Compositing
text:
Use Focus Bracketing when the subject is static and depth of field is not enough in one frame.
Recommended:
- Macro subjects.
- Water drops.
- Flowers.
- Coins.
- Jewelry.
- Watches.
- Food.
- Electronics.
- Product photography.
- Architecture details.
- Landscapes with close foreground elements.

title: Focus Bracketing & In-Camera Depth Compositing
text:
Avoid Focus Bracketing when the subject is moving, the camera cannot be held steady, wind is moving important details, or the flash cannot recycle fast enough.
Avoid it for portraits unless there is a specific static setup. Eye and face movement usually make stacking unnecessary or unreliable.
Avoid using very small apertures and stacking at the same time unless there is a deliberate reason. Diffraction can soften every frame in the stack.

title: Focus Bracketing & In-Camera Depth Compositing
text: Canon Focus Bracketing settings control how far the camera travels through the subject and how reliable the overlap is between frames.

title: Focus Bracketing & In-Camera Depth Compositing
text:
What it controls: the maximum number of frames the camera will capture in the bracket.
Effect of increasing the value: more depth coverage and less chance that the sequence stops before the far edge is sharp.
Effect of decreasing the value: less time, less storage, and faster processing.
Typical values:
  Subject   Typical Number of Shots  
  ---   ---  
  Very shallow macro   100-300+  
  Flowers   30-80  
  Products   20-60  
  Landscape   3-20  
  Architecture   5-15  
  Portrait   Rarely needed  
Common mistakes:
- Too few shots leave the far edge soft.
- Too many shots waste time and storage.
- Using one shot count for every subject.
Recommended starting point: choose more shots than you think you need for the first test stack, then reduce after reviewing the last useful sharp frame.

title: Focus Bracketing & In-Camera Depth Compositing
text:
What it controls: how far the focus plane moves between frames.
Focus Increment is the most important setting. It does not represent a fixed distance in inches or millimeters. Its real-world effect changes with aperture, magnification, subject distance, and lens behavior.
Relationship to depth of field:
- Wider aperture: shallower depth of field, smaller increment needed.
- Smaller aperture: deeper depth of field, larger increment possible.
- Higher magnification: shallower depth of field, smaller increment needed.
- Closer subject: shallower depth of field, smaller increment needed.
- Greater subject distance: deeper depth of field, larger increment possible.
Small increment:
- More overlap.
- Better stacking reliability.
- More images.
- Slower workflow.
Large increment:
- Fewer images.
- Faster capture.
- Less storage.
- Greater chance of focus gaps.
Practical starting points:
  Subject   Suggested Increment  
  ---   ---  
  1:1 Macro   1-2  
  Insects   2-3  
  Flowers   3-5  
  Product Photography   3-5  
  Food   4-6  
  Landscape   5-8  
  Architecture   5-10  
These are starting points, not fixed rules. If the stack shows bands of softness, reduce the increment. If every frame is nearly identical, increase the increment.
Common mistakes:
- Using a large increment for 1:1 macro.
- Assuming the increment means the same thing with every lens.
- Changing aperture without adjusting increment.
- Starting focus in the middle of the subject instead of the nearest important detail.
Recommended starting point: increment 1-2 for true macro, 3-5 for close product work, and 5-8 for landscape.

title: Focus Bracketing & In-Camera Depth Compositing
text:
What it controls: whether the camera attempts to reduce visible exposure jumps across the bracket.
Effect of enabling it: helps make frames more consistent when natural light shifts during the sequence.
Effect of disabling it: keeps controlled studio lighting untouched and avoids unnecessary camera intervention.
Typical values:
- On for changing natural light.
- Usually Off for controlled studio lighting.
Common mistakes:
- Leaving it Off outdoors when clouds are moving quickly.
- Turning it On in a controlled flash setup and expecting it to fix inconsistent flash recycle.
Recommended starting point: On outdoors, Off in studio.

title: Focus Bracketing & In-Camera Depth Compositing
text:
What it controls: whether the camera creates a stacked composite from the bracketed images.
How it works: the camera analyzes sharp areas from each frame, aligns the stack where possible, and blends the sharp regions into one composite.
Advantages:
- Fast review in the field.
- Useful for simple subjects.
- Good for quick documentation.
Limitations:
- Less control over halos and alignment.
- Less retouching flexibility.
- Can struggle with movement and focus breathing.
- External software usually produces better results for serious macro, product, and landscape work.
Common mistakes:
- Treating the in-camera composite as the only file worth keeping.
- Expecting it to fix movement, wind, or poor overlap.
Recommended starting point: use in-camera compositing for quick checks; keep source frames for final stacking.

title: Focus Bracketing & In-Camera Depth Compositing
text:
How much depth needs to be covered?
- Small depth: use fewer shots.
- Large depth: use more shots.
How large is the subject?
- Tiny macro subject: small increment and many shots.
- Larger product or landscape: larger increment and fewer shots.
How close am I?
- Very close: smaller increment.
- Farther away: larger increment.
What aperture am I using?
- f/5.6-f/8 macro: small increment.
- f/8-f/11 product or landscape: moderate increment.
- f/16 or smaller: watch for diffraction; stacking may be better at a wider aperture.
Choose Focus Increment.
Estimate Number of Shots.
Review the first stack.
Adjust.

title: Focus Bracketing & In-Camera Depth Compositing
text:
Use this field process:
1. Decide how much physical depth must be sharp.
2. Check how close you are to the subject.
3. Estimate magnification. The closer the lens is to macro range, the smaller the increment should be.
4. Choose an aperture that balances sharpness and depth. f/8 is a strong starting point for many macro and product stacks.
5. Choose Focus Increment.
6. Choose Number of Shots.
7. Run a test stack.
8. Review the first and last frames.
9. Adjust increment or shot count.
Worked examples:
- Water drops at f/8 with a 100 mm macro lens: start at increment 1 and 150 shots. If the rear edge is still soft, increase shots. If many final frames add no detail, reduce shots.
- Flower at f/8: start at increment 4 and 50 shots. If petal edges show focus gaps, reduce increment to 3 or increase shots.
- Product watch at f/8: start at increment 3 and 80 shots. Use tripod and controlled light. If bracelet links show gaps, reduce increment.
- Landscape with close foreground flowers at f/8: start at increment 5 and 15 shots. If the background is already sharp by frame 8, reduce shots next time.
- Architecture detail at f/8: start at increment 7 and 8 shots. Use more shots only if close foreground detail is included.

title: Focus Bracketing & In-Camera Depth Compositing
text:
Profile / Subject   Aperture   Focus Increment   Number of Shots   Notes  
  ---   ---   ---   ---   ---  
  Macro   f/8   1-3   80-200   Use tripod when possible  
  Water Drops   f/8   1   150   Flash recycle and timing matter  
  Landscape close foreground   f/8   5-8   5-20   Start near foreground detail  
  Product   f/8   3-5   20-80   Controlled light and tripod  
  Flowers   f/8   3-5   30-80   Watch wind  
  Insects   f/8   2-3   100-200   Subject movement is the main risk  
  Architecture   f/8   5-10   5-15   Usually fewer frames than macro

title: Focus Bracketing & In-Camera Depth Compositing
text:
Canon R5 Focus Bracketing starts from the current focus position and moves focus farther away. Focus carefully on the nearest important detail before starting the sequence.
If using in-camera Depth Compositing, keep expectations practical. It is useful for fast review and simple stacks, but external software usually provides better alignment, retouching, and artifact control.
Some Canon lenses change framing noticeably as focus shifts. This focus breathing can make stacks harder to align, especially with product and macro subjects.

title: Focus Bracketing & In-Camera Depth Compositing
text:
- Start with the nearest important detail.
- Use a tripod for serious stacks.
- Use f/8 as a practical first aperture for many macro/product stacks.
- Review the first and last frame to confirm the full depth range was covered.
- If there are focus gaps, lower the increment or increase shot count.
- If many frames are redundant, raise the increment or reduce shot count.
- Use controlled lighting when possible.
- Protect the subject from wind.
- Leave extra room around the subject for alignment and cropping.

title: Focus Bracketing & In-Camera Depth Compositing
text:
- Starting focus too far into the subject.
- Using too few shots.
- Using an increment that is too large for macro.
- Using f/22 and getting diffraction-soft source frames.
- Letting flash recycle time lag behind the bracket sequence.
- Ignoring subject movement.
- Forgetting focus breathing.
- Keeping hundreds of unnecessary frames.

  Insects   f/8   2   120

  Coins   f/8   3   60

text:
Lens   Magnification   Aperture   Increment   Starting Shots

title: Focus Bracketing & In-Camera Depth Compositing
text:
RF 100 mm Macro: excellent for high-magnification stacking. At 1:1 or higher, use very small increments and many shots.
EF 100 mm Macro: similar field logic to the RF macro, though focus behavior and breathing may differ when adapted.
EF 100-400L: useful for close telephoto detail, flowers, compressed landscape details, and larger products. Use moderate increments because magnification is lower than true macro but depth can still be shallow at close focus.
Wide-angle lenses: landscapes often need fewer frames. Use Focus Bracketing when the foreground is close and important, not for every scenic view.
Working distance and magnification matter more than lens name. The closer and larger the subject appears, the smaller the increment should be.

title: Focus Bracketing & In-Camera Depth Compositing
text:
- Missing focus slices: usually caused by an increment that is too large.
- Halo artifacts: usually caused by movement, focus breathing, or difficult high-contrast edges.
- Subject movement: common with insects, flowers, water, and outdoor foregrounds.
- Wind: the main outdoor stacking problem for flowers and foreground landscape elements.
- Flash recycle issues: missed or underexposed frames appear when flash cannot recharge between shots.
- Focus breathing: framing changes as focus moves, especially at close distances.
- Too many images: slows sorting, stacking, storage, and backup.
- Too few images: leaves gaps or stops before the far edge is sharp.
- Diffraction: very small apertures soften every frame before stacking starts.

title: Focus Bracketing & In-Camera Depth Compositing
text:
Problem   Likely Cause   Recommended Solution  
  ---   ---   ---  
  Missing focus slices   Increment too large   Lower increment and reshoot  
  Far edge still soft   Too few shots   Increase shot count  
  Near edge soft   Started focus too far away   Start on nearest important detail  
  Halo artifacts   Blend struggled with edges   Use external software and retouch  
  Subject movement   Wind or living subject moved   Shield subject, wait, or abandon stack  
  Flash misses frames   Recycle too slow   Lower flash power, raise ISO, or slow sequence  
  Framing changes   Focus breathing   Leave extra crop room and use external software  
  Too many images   Shot count too high   Reduce shots after test stack  
  Diffraction softness   Aperture too small   Use wider aperture and stack more frames

title: Focus Bracketing & In-Camera Depth Compositing
text:
Tripod?
- No: use only for casual stacks; keep shots low and expect alignment issues.
- Yes: continue.
Macro?
- Yes: start increment 1-3 and 80-200 shots.
- No: continue.
Product or flower?
- Yes: start increment 3-5 and 30-80 shots.
- No: continue.
Landscape?
- Foreground close: start increment 5-8 and 5-20 shots.
- No close foreground: Focus Bracketing may not be needed.
Landscape decision tree:
Landscape?
- No: use the subject-specific table.
- Yes: continue.
Foreground close?
- No: single frame may be enough.
- Yes: continue.
Need foreground and background critically sharp?
- No: stop down moderately and shoot one frame.
- Yes: use Focus Bracketing at f/8, increment 5-8, 5-20 shots.
Flash decision tree:
Using flash?
- No: continue normally.
- Yes: test recycle time.
Flash keeps up with every frame?
- Yes: continue.
- No: lower flash power, raise ISO, slow the sequence, or reduce the number of frames.

title: Image Stabilization
text:
Explain how image stabilization works on the Canon EOS R5 and when to use Mode 1, Mode 2, Mode 3, or Off.
On the Canon EOS R5, image stabilization can come from three places:
- Lens IS in supported lenses.
- IBIS (In-Body Image Stabilization) in the camera.
- Coordinated IS when a supported RF lens and the camera body work together.

title: Image Stabilization
text:
Image stabilization reduces blur from camera movement. It does not freeze subject motion; shutter speed still controls motion blur from the subject.
The camera offers several stabilization behaviors depending on the lens and shooting mode.
  Mode   What it stabilizes   Best use  
  ---   ---   ---  
  Mode 1 (Continuous IS)   Vertical + horizontal movement   General photography, portraits, landscapes, travel, handheld macro, low-light handheld work  
  Mode 2 (Panning IS)   Vertical movement only during horizontal pans   Motorsports, airshows, birds flying steadily across the frame, cyclists, running subjects while panning  
  Mode 3 (Exposure-only IS)   Stabilization activates fully during exposure   Erratic action where a stabilized viewfinder can make tracking harder, including birds, wildlife, and sports  
  Off   No stabilization   Solid tripod work, multi-minute exposures, astrophotography, very high shutter speeds, and certain long exposures

title: Image Stabilization
text:
Lens IS moves lens elements to counter camera shake. IBIS moves the camera sensor. With compatible RF lenses, the R5 coordinates lens IS and IBIS automatically, so the main field decision is usually the IS mode or whether stabilization should be off.
Mode 1 stabilizes continuously. Mode 2 ignores intentional panning movement in one direction. Mode 3 keeps the view less stabilized while tracking and applies stabilization during the exposure.

title: Image Stabilization
text:
- Mode 1 is the best default for most handheld photography.
- Mode 2 supports intentional motion blur while panning.
- Mode 3 can make erratic subjects easier to track.
- Turning IS off avoids stabilization drift during some tripod and long-exposure work.
- Coordinated IS can improve handheld results with supported RF lenses.

title: Image Stabilization
text:
- Stabilization cannot stop subject motion.
- Mode 1 can create a floating viewfinder feel with erratic subjects.
- Mode 2 is specialized and should not be used as a general default.
- Stabilization can be unnecessary or counterproductive on a solid tripod.
- Some lenses do not provide all modes.

title: Image Stabilization
text:
Use Mode 1 for landscapes, portraits, street photography, travel, handheld macro, and low-light handheld work. This is the default mode and should be used most of the time.
Use Mode 2 for intentional panning, such as a race car at 1/60 sec where the background blurs while the car stays sharp.
Use Mode 3 for birds in flight, football, soccer, basketball, and wildlife moving unpredictably. Many wildlife photographers leave long telephotos in Mode 3.
Turn IS off for solid tripod work, multi-minute exposures, astrophotography, and situations where shutter speeds are already very fast, such as 1/2000-1/4000 sec.

title: Image Stabilization
text:
Do not leave stabilization on automatically for every tripod setup. Modern Canon RF lenses generally detect tripod use well, but for completely static tripod shots, turning stabilization off can still be beneficial.
Do not rely on IS to solve subject blur. If the subject is moving, raise shutter speed or change timing.
Avoid Mode 2 unless you are intentionally panning.

title: Image Stabilization
text:
If handheld and the subject is mostly static, use Mode 1.
If intentionally panning, use Mode 2.
If tracking fast erratic subjects, use Mode 3.
If on a solid tripod for static or long-exposure work, turn IS off.
If using an RF lens with IS, let the R5 coordinate lens IS and IBIS; choose the mode based on the subject.

text:

  Birds in Flight   Mode 3 by default; Mode 2 for deliberate steady pans   Mode 3 by default; Mode 2 for smooth lateral pans  
  Sports   Mode 3   Mode 3  
  Panning (cars, aircraft, cyclists)   Mode 2   Mode 2

title: Image Stabilization
text:
With RF lenses that include IS, the R5 automatically coordinates lens and body stabilization. You do not normally choose lens IS versus IBIS separately in the field.
Some lenses expose a physical IS switch and mode selector. Lens controls can determine whether the camera menu offers the expected options.

title: Image Stabilization
text:
For the Canon EF 100-400mm f/4.5-5.6L IS II USM, commonly adapted to the R5 with a Canon EF-EOS R adapter, stabilization is controlled by the switch on the lens.
When this lens is used on the R5 with the Canon adapter, the lens IS and the camera's IBIS work together. In the field, treat the lens IS mode switch as the primary stabilization mode choice.
  Lens IS Mode   Purpose   Best use  
  ---   ---   ---  
  Mode 1   Stabilizes in all directions   General wildlife, perched birds, landscapes, travel, handheld telephoto  
  Mode 2   Panning mode   Birds flying smoothly across the frame, motorsports, cyclists, runners  
  Mode 3   Stabilization only during exposure   Birds in flight, erratic wildlife, field sports, fast action  
  Off   No stabilization   Rigid tripod work, very long exposures, or very fast shutter speeds where IS adds little practical benefit  
Use Mode 1 for static or slow-moving subjects: perched birds, wildlife at rest, zoo photography, travel, handheld landscapes, and general telephoto work.
Use Mode 2 for intentional panning. The lens detects the panning direction and stabilizes only perpendicular to that movement. This is the right choice for horizontal pans of birds in flight, airshows, motorsports, cyclists, and running animals.
Use Mode 3 for action that changes direction quickly. The viewfinder remains largely unstabilized while tracking, and full stabilization is applied only when the shutter is released. This is often preferred for birds in flight, erratic wildlife, sports, and subjects that are difficult to keep in the frame.
Many wildlife photographers leave this lens in Mode 3 for unpredictable subjects because it makes tracking feel more natural.
For completely static tripod work, long exposures, and astrophotography, turning stabilization off remains a good practice.
For this reference system, treat Mode 1 as the default, Mode 2 as the dedicated panning mode, and Mode 3 as the default for fast action.

title: Image Stabilization
text:
- Use Mode 1 as the everyday handheld default.
- Use Mode 3 for long telephoto wildlife and unpredictable action.
- Use Mode 2 only when you are deliberately panning.
- Recheck IS after switching between handheld and tripod work.
- Treat stabilization as camera-shake reduction, not a substitute for shutter speed.

title: Image Stabilization
text:
- Leaving Mode 1 on for erratic action when Mode 3 would track better.
- Using Mode 2 for non-panning subjects.
- Leaving IS on during long static tripod exposures.
- Expecting stabilization to freeze subject movement.
- Forgetting that some lenses do not provide Mode 2 or Mode 3.

title: Lens Capabilities
text: Handheld works well in good light. Use a monopod or gimbal for long sessions, long waits, or smoother subject tracking. Use a tripod for static wildlife, landscapes, moon shots, and careful compositions. On a locked tripod, turn stabilization off unless you are actively tracking.

title: Lens Capabilities
text:
The EF 100-400L IS II is the main lens in this kit where the 1.4x extender makes sense. Put the EF 1.4x extender between the EF lens and the EF-EOS R adapter, then mount the adapter to the R5.
With a 1.4x extender, this lens becomes 140-560mm f/6.3-8. Expect more reach, less light, slightly slower AF, and more need for steady support. It is best for perched birds, distant wildlife, moon/detail shots, and bright outdoor subjects. Avoid it for low light, fast erratic action, or heat shimmer unless reach matters more than speed.
The EF-EOS R control ring adapter does not change focal length or aperture. Use the control ring for a high-value field setting such as exposure compensation, ISO, or aperture, depending on how you like to shoot.

title: Long Exposure & Night Photography
text:
Explain the Canon R5 choices that matter for long exposures, night photography, fireworks, waterfalls, light painting, Milky Way images, and other tripod-based low-light work.
This appendix is the single source of truth for Long Exposure Noise Reduction (LENR). Profiles should reference this appendix rather than duplicating LENR explanations.
Table of contents:
- Long Exposure Noise Reduction (LENR)
- Processing Time
- AUTO Setting
- Decision Guide
- Recommended Settings by Profile
- Cross References

title: Long Exposure & Night Photography
text: Long exposure settings control how the R5 handles multi-second captures, sensor heat, tripod stability, shutter behavior, stabilization, and processing delays.

title: Long Exposure & Night Photography
text:
Long Exposure Noise Reduction performs a second exposure, called a dark frame, immediately after the original exposure with the shutter closed.
The camera compares the dark frame with the original exposure and removes or reduces:
- Hot pixels.
- Thermal noise.
- Amp glow.
- Fixed-pattern sensor noise.
LENR is different from High ISO Noise Reduction. High ISO Noise Reduction targets high-sensitivity noise; LENR targets artifacts created or revealed by longer exposure times.

title: Long Exposure & Night Photography
text:
The camera uses dark frame subtraction. If the original exposure is 30 seconds, the R5 records the main image, then spends roughly another 30 seconds recording a dark frame. The dark frame contains sensor noise patterns but no scene detail, allowing the camera to subtract those artifacts.
During the dark-frame exposure, the camera cannot take another photograph.

title: Long Exposure & Night Photography
text:
LENR effectively doubles capture time.
  Exposure   Camera Busy Time  
  ---   ---  
  5 sec   10 sec  
  15 sec   30 sec  
  30 sec   60 sec  
  1 minute   2 minutes  
  5 minutes   10 minutes  
The camera may display BUSY while the dark frame is being captured or processed.

title: Long Exposure & Night Photography
text:
- Reduces hot pixels.
- Reduces thermal noise.
- Reduces fixed-pattern sensor noise.
- Helps control amp glow.
- Improves single long exposures.
- Is especially useful on warm nights.
- Produces cleaner night photographs directly from the camera.

title: Long Exposure & Night Photography
text:
- Doubles shooting time.
- Makes the camera unavailable during processing.
- Increases battery usage.
- Can cause missed opportunities.
- Is not efficient for image stacking workflows.
- Can interrupt fireworks, lightning, time-lapse, and changing-light sequences.

title: Long Exposure & Night Photography
text:
Turn LENR On for important one-off long exposures where maximum single-frame image quality is more important than speed.
Recommended On:
- Single night landscapes.
- Single cityscapes.
- Single waterfall exposures.
- Single light-painting images.
- Single fireworks exposures.
- Single Milky Way exposures.
- Any important one-off long exposure where maximum image quality is desired.
For exposures under a few seconds, the benefit is usually minimal. LENR becomes more valuable as exposure duration and sensor heat increase.

title: Long Exposure & Night Photography
text:
Turn LENR Off for workflows where many images will be captured or combined later.
Recommended Off:
- Focus bracketing.
- Exposure bracketing.
- Time-lapse.
- Astrophotography stacking.
- Star trails.
- Multiple waterfall exposures.
- Burst long exposures.
- Waterdrop flash work.
- Any workflow where many images will be combined later.
Post-processing software generally provides better results for stacked images than applying LENR to every frame in camera. If shooting many long exposures, disable LENR and remove hot pixels during post-processing or with a planned dark-frame workflow.

title: Long Exposure & Night Photography
text:
AUTO lets the camera decide when to apply Long Exposure Noise Reduction.
AUTO is suitable for general photography and travel, but the user may experience unexpected processing delays. If timing matters, choose On or Off deliberately instead of leaving the decision to the camera.

title: Long Exposure & Night Photography
text:
Long exposure?
- No: Off or Auto.
- Yes: continue.
More than one exposure?
- Yes: Off.
- No: continue.
Maximum single-frame image quality required?
- Yes: On.
- No: Auto.
If timing matters more than maximum single-frame cleanup, choose Off.

title: Long Exposure & Night Photography
text:
Photography Type   Recommended Setting  
  ---   ---  
  Normal   Auto  
  Travel   Auto  
  Landscape (single exposure)   On  
  Landscape (multiple exposures)   Off  
  Night Landscape   On  
  Milky Way (single image)   On  
  Milky Way (stacking)   Off  
  Star Trails   Off  
  Waterfalls   Off  
  Light Painting   On  
  Fireworks (single exposure)   On  
  Fireworks (sequence)   Off  
  Focus Bracketing   Off  
  Wildlife   Off  
  Birds in Flight   Off  
  Sports   Off  
  Waterdrops   Off

title: Long Exposure & Night Photography
text:
On the Canon EOS R5, Long Exposure Noise Reduction can affect RAW and JPEG workflows because the correction is applied by the camera, not merely stored as a post-processing suggestion.
The project baseline should avoid forcing LENR On. Use Auto for general/travel workflows when convenience matters, On for single critical long exposures, and Off for stacked or repeated-capture workflows.
LENR is separate from High ISO Noise Reduction. Do not use LENR as a general high-ISO cleanup tool.

title: Long Exposure & Night Photography
text:
- For exposures under a few seconds, the benefit is usually minimal.
- LENR is most valuable for long exposures where sensor heating becomes significant.
- If shooting many long exposures, disable LENR and remove hot pixels during post-processing.
- Expect the camera to display BUSY while capturing the dark frame.
- Decide before the sequence starts; changing mid-shoot can make files inconsistent.
- Watch battery life during long exposure sessions.

title: Long Exposure & Night Photography
text:
- Leaving LENR On after a night shoot.
- Using LENR for waterdrop flash work.
- Confusing LENR with High ISO Noise Reduction.
- Missing fireworks or lightning because the camera is busy processing.
- Applying LENR to every frame in a stacking workflow.
- Forgetting that processing delay scales with exposure length.

title: Flash Photography
text: Explain Canon R5 flash concepts and practical choices for E-TTL, manual flash, sync modes, and triggers.

title: Flash Photography
text: Flash adds controlled light to a scene, either as primary light, fill, or a timed creative accent.

title: Flash Photography
text: E-TTL meters a preflash automatically. Manual flash uses a fixed power. Sync modes determine when the flash fires relative to the shutter.

title: Flash Photography
text:
- E-TTL adapts quickly.
- Manual flash is consistent.
- Flash Exposure Compensation fine-tunes automatic flash.
- High Speed Sync allows wider apertures in bright light.
- Rear Curtain Sync places motion trails behind subjects.

title: Flash Photography
text:
- HSS reduces power.
- E-TTL can vary between frames.
- Manual flash requires distance/power adjustment.
- Multiple flash and wireless flash add setup complexity.

title: Flash Photography
text: Use fill flash for people and travel. Use bounce flash indoors. Use manual flash for repeatable portraits, macro, and products. Use wireless flash for multi-light control.

title: Flash Photography
text: Avoid flash when it is prohibited, distracting, too weak for the distance, or incompatible with the selected shutter mode.

title: Flash Photography
text: Fast event: E-TTL. Controlled setup: Manual. Bright daylight wide aperture: HSS. Motion trail: Rear Curtain Sync. Remote timing: external trigger or Pluto trigger.

title: Flash Photography
text:
- People: E-TTL fill or bounce flash.
- Macro: manual flash with diffuser.
- Travel: small fill flash when allowed.
- Fireworks: generally no flash for distant bursts.

title: Flash Photography
text: Canon Speedlite systems support E-TTL, wireless control, Flash Exposure Compensation, and HSS. Flash generally requires Mechanical shutter on the R5.

title: Flash Photography
text: Bounce when possible. Start with negative Flash Exposure Compensation for subtle fill. Check recycle time.

title: Flash Photography
text:
- Expecting flash to light distant subjects.
- Forgetting shutter sync limits.
- Using HSS unnecessarily.
- Leaving Flash Exposure Compensation from a prior scene.

title: Flash Photography
text:
- Profiles: Fireworks, Macro, People, Travel.
- Settings: flash mode, shutter mode, metering, exposure compensation.
- Related appendices: Electronic vs EFCS vs Mechanical Shutter, Metering Modes.

title: Custom Controls
text: Define recommended Canon R5 control customization patterns for faster field operation.

title: Custom Controls
text: Custom controls assign camera functions to buttons, dials, the joystick, control ring, and recall functions.

title: Custom Controls
text: The R5 allows button customization for M-Fn, AF-ON, AE Lock, Control Ring, joystick behavior, and Register/Recall Shooting Function.

title: Custom Controls
text:
- Faster access to frequent controls.
- Less menu diving.
- Better separation of focus, exposure, and shutter release.
- Profile-specific layouts can support different subjects.

title: Custom Controls
text:
- Too much customization can be hard to remember.
- Shared cameras become confusing.
- Accidental button presses can change behavior unexpectedly.

title: Custom Controls
text: Use AF-ON for back-button focus, joystick for AF point movement, Control Ring for exposure compensation or ISO, and Register/Recall for temporary action settings.

title: Custom Controls
text: Avoid complex layouts before a critical shoot. Avoid assigning rarely used functions to prime controls.

title: Custom Controls
text: Frequent action: assign to button. Needs precision: joystick or dial. Temporary override: Register/Recall. Rare function: leave in menu.

title: Custom Controls
text:
- Wildlife: AF-ON, animal detection access, recall faster shutter/drive.
- Landscape: Control Ring for exposure compensation, stable focus controls.
- Sports: recall action settings and AF mode changes.
- Travel: simple layout with exposure compensation and AF area access.

title: Custom Controls
text: Canon R5 Custom Controls and Register/Recall Shooting Function are powerful but should be documented per profile.

title: Custom Controls
text: Keep one consistent base layout. Add only one or two profile-specific changes.

title: Custom Controls
text:
- Over-customizing.
- Forgetting what a button does.
- Changing controls without updating profile notes.

title: Custom Controls
text:
- Profiles: Birds in Flight, Landscape, Sports, Travel, Wildlife.

title: Drive Modes
text: Explain Canon R5 drive modes and when to use single, continuous, silent, and self-timer options.

title: Drive Modes
text: Drive mode controls how many frames the camera captures per shutter press and whether delays or silent capture modes are used.

title: Drive Modes
text: Single Shot captures one frame. Low Continuous, High Continuous, and High+ Continuous capture bursts at increasing rates. Silent modes depend on electronic shutter behavior. Self Timer delays capture.

title: Drive Modes
text:
- Single Shot encourages timing and conserves storage.
- Low Continuous supports controlled sequences.
- High and High+ Continuous capture peak action.
- Silent modes reduce disturbance.
- Self Timer reduces camera shake.

title: Drive Modes
text:
- High burst rates create many files.
- Silent/electronic drive can have rolling shutter limits.
- Continuous drive can hide timing mistakes.
- Self Timer is too slow for action.

title: Drive Modes
text: Use Single Shot for static scenes. Use Low Continuous for portraits or mild movement. Use High/High+ for action, birds, wildlife, and sports. Use Self Timer for tripod work.

title: Drive Modes
text: Avoid High+ when storage, buffer, or editing load matters. Avoid silent electronic drive with fast motion or flickering light.

title: Drive Modes
text: Static: Single Shot. Gentle movement: Low Continuous. Action: High or High+. Tripod: Self Timer or remote. Need silence: Silent mode if shutter limitations are acceptable.

title: Drive Modes
text:
- Birds in Flight: High Speed Continuous+.
- Camera Defaults: Single Shooting.
- Sports: High Continuous or High+.
- Wildlife: High Speed Continuous for behavior/action.

title: Drive Modes
text: On the Canon R5, drive mode interacts with shutter mode, buffer, card speed, and subject movement.

title: Drive Modes
text: Use short bursts. Match drive mode to the moment, not the subject category alone.

title: Drive Modes
text:
- Leaving High+ on for static scenes.
- Forgetting silent mode limitations.
- Filling the buffer before peak action.

title: Drive Modes
text:
- Profiles: Birds in Flight, Camera Defaults, Sports, Wildlife.

title: R5 Quick Reference
text: Use this quick reference when a card setting is not obvious, especially AF Operation, Subject Detection, Eye Detection, AF Method, Focus Mode, Drive Mode, Metering Mode, Shutter Speed, Image Stabilization Mode, IBIS, Lens IS, Shutter Type, Focus Bracketing, or Long Exposure Noise Reduction. Basic exposure terms such as Mode, Aperture, ISO, and Auto ISO Max are intentionally kept out of the main quick reference.

title: R5 Quick Reference
text: Canon EOS R5 shooting modes define how exposure decisions are divided between the photographer and the camera.

title: R5 Quick Reference
section: Choosing Shooting Mode: P vs Fv vs Av/Tv/M
text:
Use P for simple Program AE when you want low-friction general shooting and are happy for the camera to choose shutter speed and aperture.
Use Fv as the flexible R5 general-purpose mode. It can behave like P, but lets you quickly take control of shutter speed, aperture, ISO, or exposure compensation without changing modes. In this project, consider both P and Fv for general shooting; Fv is often better for an experienced R5 user because it gives selective control while staying fast.
Use Av when aperture and depth of field are the main decision, such as landscapes, portraits, perched birds, natural-light macro, and tripod/still scenes.
Use Tv when shutter speed is the main decision, such as birds in flight, sports, active wildlife, and fast action.
Use M when controlled or repeatable exposure matters more than camera automation, such as waterdrops, flash macro, studio flash, and repeatable night setups.
Use Bulb only for long manual exposures where you directly control how long the shutter stays open.
  Icon   Setting   Canon Name   What it Does  
 --- --- --- --- 
  !A+   A+   Scene Intelligent Auto   Camera analyzes the scene and sets optimum settings automatically.  
  !Fv   Fv   Flexible-priority AE   Allows shutter speed, aperture, ISO, and exposure compensation to be manual or automatic.  
  !P   P   Program AE   Camera automatically sets shutter speed and aperture for subject brightness.  
  !Tv   Tv   Shutter-priority AE   You set shutter speed; the camera sets aperture for standard exposure.  
  !Av   Av   Aperture-priority AE   You set aperture; the camera sets shutter speed for standard exposure.  
  !M   M   Manual exposure   You set shutter speed and aperture and judge exposure from the exposure indicator.  
  !BULB   BULB   Bulb   Shutter remains open while the shutter button is held for long exposures.  
  !C1   C1   Custom shooting mode 1   Recalls registered camera settings.  
  !C2   C2   Custom shooting mode 2   Recalls registered camera settings.  
  !C3   C3   Custom shooting mode 3   Recalls registered camera settings.  
**Camera Menu:** MODE button, then Main dial. Custom modes are registered under Set-up > Custom shooting mode (C1-C3).
**Quick Menu:** Exposure mode is normally changed with the MODE button, not the Q screen.

title: R5 Quick Reference
text:
These features support manual focus precision and depth-of-field workflows. Keep the detailed setup in the focus appendix; cards should only show the setting when it matters.
  Icon   Setting   Canon Name   What it Does  
 --- --- --- --- 
  !Focus Bracketing   Focus Bracketing   Focus Bracketing   Captures a sequence while automatically shifting focus after each shot.  
  !Focus Guide   Focus Guide   Focus Guide   Displays a guide frame showing direction and amount of manual focus adjustment.  
  !MF Peaking   MF Peaking   MF Peaking (Outline Emphasis)   Displays in-focus edges in color to make manual focusing easier.  
**Camera Menu:** Shooting menu > Focus bracketing; AF menu > Focus guide; AF menu > MF peaking settings.
**Quick Menu:** Menu only.

title: R5 Quick Reference
text:
Use Servo AF for movement, One Shot AF for static subjects, and Manual Focus when focus must stay locked or autofocus would hunt.
  Icon   Setting   What it Does  
 --- --- --- 
  !AF   Servo AF   Continuously updates focus for moving subjects.  
  !AF   One-Shot AF   Locks focus for static or slow subjects.  
  !MF   Manual Focus   Stops autofocus hunting and keeps focus under manual control.  
**Camera Menu:** AF1 > AF operation; for Manual Focus use the lens AF/MF switch or AF1 > Focus mode when shown.
**Quick Menu:** Q screen / M-Fn for AF Operation. Manual Focus is usually the lens switch, not Q.

title: R5 Quick Reference
text:
Use People, Animals, or Vehicles when the camera can recognize the subject. Use None when a smaller AF area or manual focus should control the point.
  Icon   Setting   What it Does  
 --- --- --- 
  !People   People   Prioritizes human faces, heads, and eyes.  
  !Animals   Animals   Prioritizes animals and birds when recognized.  
  !Vehicles   Vehicles   Prioritizes racing cars and motorcycles when recognized.  
  !None   None   Prevents subject recognition from pulling focus away from the chosen area.  
**Camera Menu:** AF1 > Subject to detect.
**Quick Menu:** Menu only.

title: R5 Quick Reference
text:
Enable it when Face + Tracking and subject detection are useful. It is not useful when AF is manual or when the selected AF method cannot use eye tracking.
  Icon   Setting   What it Does  
 --- --- --- 
  !Eye detection   Enable   Lets compatible AF methods prioritize the subject's eye.  
  !Eye detection   Disable   Prevents eye priority when the AF area or subject choice should stay simpler.  
  !Eye detection   Not shown   Usually means AF is manual or the AF method cannot use eye tracking.  
**Camera Menu:** AF1 > Eye detection.
**Quick Menu:** AF point selection button, then INFO toggle when available.

title: R5 Quick Reference
text:
Face + Tracking is for subject-aware tracking. 1-Point AF is precise and predictable. Spot AF is smaller and more exact. Expand AF Area helps with action when tracking may lose the subject.
  Icon   Setting   What it Does  
 --- --- --- 
  !Face+Tracking   Face + Tracking   Tracks recognized subjects across the frame.  
  !1-point AF   1-Point AF   Gives predictable focus control on one selected point.  
  !Spot AF   Spot AF   Uses a smaller point for very precise placement.  
  !Expand AF area   Expand AF Area   Starts from one point but uses surrounding points to help with action.  
  screen-only   Not Used   AF method does not matter because autofocus is not controlling focus.  
**Camera Menu:** AF1 > AF method.
**Quick Menu:** AF point selection button, then M-Fn.

title: R5 Quick Reference
text:
AF lets the camera drive focus. MF leaves focus under manual control from the lens or camera setting. When Focus Mode is MF, AF Method, Subject Detection, and Eye Detection do not help focusing.
  Icon   Setting   What it Does  
 --- --- --- 
  !AF   AF   Lets the camera or lens drive focus.  
  !MF   MF   Leaves focus under manual control from the lens or camera setting.  
**Camera Menu:** Lens AF/MF switch; AF1 > Focus mode when shown.
**Quick Menu:** Lens switch; usually not a Q item.

title: R5 Quick Reference
text:
Single Shooting is deliberate. Low or High Speed Continuous helps with expression, motion, and timing. High Speed Continuous+ maximizes capture rate but creates more files.
  Icon   Setting   What it Does  
 --- --- --- 
  !Single shooting   Single Shooting   Takes one frame per shutter press for deliberate work.  
  !Low-speed continuous shooting   Low Speed Continuous   Captures short bursts without creating too many files.  
  !High-speed continuous shooting   High Speed Continuous   Increases capture rate for movement and changing expressions.  
  !High-speed continuous shooting plus   High Speed Continuous+   Maximizes capture rate for fast action, with more files to sort later.  
  !10 sec. self-timer   Self-timer: 10 sec.   Delays capture for tripod work, group frames, or remote release timing.  
  !2 sec. self-timer   Self-timer: 2 sec.   Delays capture briefly to let tripod shake settle after pressing the shutter.  
**Camera Menu:** M-Fn drive mode item.
**Quick Menu:** Q screen / M-Fn.

title: R5 Quick Reference
text:
Evaluative is the safest general default because it reads the whole scene and works well with modern Canon exposure logic. Spot or Partial metering is useful when a small subject or bright background should control exposure.
  Icon   Setting   What it Does  
 --- --- --- 
  !Evaluative metering   Evaluative   Reads the whole scene and is the safest general default.  
  !Partial metering   Partial   Meters a central area when the background should matter less.  
  !Spot metering   Spot   Meters a very small area when one subject tone must control exposure.  
  !Center-weighted average metering   Center-weighted   Emphasizes the center while still considering the whole frame.  
**Camera Menu:** Shooting menu > Metering mode.
**Quick Menu:** Q screen / M-Fn.

title: R5 Quick Reference
text:
Shutter speed controls motion. Action profiles use fast shutter speeds; long exposure profiles use slow shutter speeds.
  Setting   What it Does  
 --- --- 
  Fast shutter speed   Freezes motion for action, wildlife, or sports.  
  Slow shutter speed   Allows long exposures for fireworks, night work, or blur effects.  
  Camera-selected shutter speed   Lets the camera choose shutter speed from the exposure mode.  
**Camera Menu:** Tv or Manual exposure shutter speed control; Av/P/Fv allow the camera to choose shutter speed.
**Quick Menu:** Top dial / Q screen depending on exposure mode.

title: R5 Quick Reference
text:
Mode 1 is general handheld stabilization. Mode 2 is for panning. Mode 3 is for erratic action and stabilizes mainly during exposure. Off is safest on a solid tripod.
  Icon   Setting   What it Does  
 --- --- --- 
  !Image Stabilization   Mode 1   General handheld stabilization.  
  !Image Stabilization   Mode 2   Stabilization for deliberate panning.  
  !Image Stabilization   Mode 3   Stabilization for erratic action, mainly during exposure.  
  !Image Stabilization   Off   Best for solid tripod work or profiles where stabilization can cause problems.  
**Camera Menu:** Shooting 7 > IS (Image Stabilizer) mode, or lens IS controls. Mode 2/3 may be lens-switch settings when available.
**Quick Menu:** Menu/lens control; not usually Q.

title: R5 Quick Reference
text:
In-body image stabilization moves the camera sensor to reduce shake. It helps handheld work and should usually be off for solid tripod long exposures.
  Icon   Setting   What it Does  
 --- --- --- 
  !ON   On   Uses in-body sensor movement to reduce camera shake.  
  !OFF   Off   Prevents sensor stabilization during solid tripod or locked-down work.  
**Camera Menu:** Shooting 7 > IS (Image Stabilizer) mode.
**Quick Menu:** Menu only.

title: R5 Quick Reference
text:
Lens stabilization moves lens elements to reduce shake. With compatible lenses, Lens IS and IBIS may work together. Turn it off on a solid tripod when the profile calls for locked-down work.
  Icon   Setting   What it Does  
 --- --- --- 
  !ON   On   Uses lens stabilization to reduce shake, often coordinated with IBIS.  
  !OFF   Off   Prevents lens stabilization during solid tripod or locked-down work.  
**Camera Menu:** Lens IS switch; Shooting 7 > IS mode where available.
**Quick Menu:** Lens switch; not Q.

title: R5 Quick Reference
text:
Mechanical is the safest general default. EFCS can reduce vibration. Electronic is silent but can create rolling shutter, banding, or flash limitations.
  Setting   What it Does  
 --- --- 
  Mechanical   Safest general default with broad flash and motion compatibility.  
  EFCS   Reduces vibration while avoiding many electronic shutter tradeoffs.  
  Electronic   Silent shooting, but watch for rolling shutter, banding, and flash limits.  
**Camera Menu:** Shooting 6 > Shutter mode.
**Quick Menu:** Menu only.

title: R5 Quick Reference
text:
Focus Bracketing captures a sequence while shifting focus through the subject. Use it when one frame cannot hold enough depth of field. For full setup guidance, see Focus Bracketing & In-Camera Depth Compositing.
  Setting   What it Does  
 --- --- 
  Enable   Turns on focus-bracket capture for near-to-far focus stacks.  
  Disable   Returns the camera to normal single-focus capture.  
  Number of shots   Controls how many frames the bracket sequence captures.  
  Focus increment   Controls how far focus moves between frames.  
Recommended: create a new folder for each subject or stack so bracket sequences stay organized.
**Camera Menu:** Shooting 5 > Focus bracketing.
**Quick Menu:** Menu only.

title: R5 Quick Reference
text:
LENR takes a dark frame after a long exposure. It can clean hot pixels but doubles wait time and is usually poor for repeated captures, stacking, or fireworks sequences.
  Setting   What it Does  
 --- --- 
  Off   Avoids delay between frames and is best for sequences or stacking.  
  Auto   Lets the camera decide when dark-frame cleanup is useful.  
  On   Takes a dark frame after long exposures to reduce hot pixels, but doubles wait time.  
**Camera Menu:** Shooting 4 > Long exp. noise reduction.
**Quick Menu:** Menu only.

title: R5 Quick Reference
section: Subject Detection and AF Method Combinations
text:
Face + Tracking works best with subject and eye detection. Expand AF Area often pairs better with Subject Detection None for action where the camera should not chase the wrong subject. Manual Focus makes Subject Detection, Eye Detection, and AF Method irrelevant on the card.
  Icon   Setting   What it Does  
 --- --- --- 
  !Face+Tracking   Face + Tracking + subject detection   Best when the camera should identify and follow the subject.  
  !Expand AF area   Expand AF Area + Subject Detection None   Useful when action is hard to track and the camera might choose the wrong subject.  
  !MF   Manual Focus   Makes Subject Detection, Eye Detection, and AF Method irrelevant on the card.  
**Camera Menu:** AF1 > AF method; AF1 > Subject to detect; lens AF/MF switch or AF1 > Focus mode when shown.
**Quick Menu:** AF method via AF point selection + M-Fn. Subject Detection is menu only. Manual Focus is usually the lens switch.

title: R5 Quick Reference
text:
- Birds in Flight: Servo AF, Animals when tracking is useful, Expand AF Area, High Speed Continuous+.
- Birds Perched: Servo AF, Animals, Eye Detection, Face + Tracking, High Speed Continuous.
- Fireworks: Manual Focus, Single Shot, tripod stabilization off, long shutter target.
- Landscape: One Shot AF, 1-Point AF, handheld stabilization by default, ISO 100 unless handheld shutter speed needs help.
- Macro: One Shot AF or Manual Focus depending on subject, Spot AF when autofocus is useful.
- People: Servo AF, People, Eye Detection, Face + Tracking.
- Sports: Servo AF, Expand AF Area, High Speed Continuous+, fast shutter target.
- Travel: Baseline settings unless the subject demands a specialized profile.
- Waterdrops: Manual Focus, Single Shooting, fixed ISO, flash/trigger workflow.
- Wildlife: Servo AF, Animals, Eye Detection when available, continuous drive.

title: R5 Quick Reference
text: Canon R5 Eye Detection is tied to subject-aware AF behavior. It is most meaningful with Face + Tracking and a suitable subject type. Manual Focus bypasses AF selection behavior, so AF Method, Subject Detection, and Eye Detection are hidden from cards when AF Operation is Manual Focus.

title: R5 Quick Reference
text:
- Change one behavior at a time when troubleshooting focus.
- If the camera jumps to the wrong subject, simplify AF Method or turn Subject Detection to None.
- For handheld landscape, raise ISO before accepting camera shake.
- For tripod work, remember to review stabilization and Long Exposure Noise Reduction.

title: R5 Quick Reference
text:
- Leaving action drive mode on for deliberate static work.
- Expecting Eye Detection to work when AF Method or Manual Focus prevents it.
- Using Electronic shutter under lights without checking for banding.
- Leaving stabilization on for solid tripod long exposures.
- Using LENR during sequences where the delay causes missed shots.

text:

title: R5 Quick Reference
text: This quick reference turns compact card labels into short practical meaning. It focuses on Canon R5 behavior that affects focusing, stabilization, drive behavior, shutter behavior, and long exposure workflow.

title: R5 Quick Reference
text: The cards show merged baseline and profile settings. Some settings are always shown because they define the profile; others appear only when a profile changes them from the baseline. Card display may combine related settings, such as IBIS and Lens IS, to save space.

title: R5 Quick Reference
text:
- Helps explain why a card chooses one focusing or stabilization behavior.
- Keeps quick-reference cards compact without hiding the meaning of important choices.
- Clarifies when the camera ignores a setting, such as AF Method during Manual Focus.

title: R5 Quick Reference
text:
- It is a quick reference, not a full camera manual.
- Some subjects still require judgment when conditions change.
- Menu names can vary slightly by firmware or shooting context.

title: R5 Quick Reference
text: Do not use this quick reference to decide basic exposure values. For aperture, ISO, and Auto ISO Max, use the card value and normal photographic judgment.
